transcript.transcript: Herb Folsom interviewing Preston Phillips Johnson. Landed in Idaho 50 years ago when the sawmills were busy. People told me no come here. Sawmills, that's all there is, wouldn't be in here pretty soon, I could hardly be here. When the World War come on Canada, I lost my wad of $90, 000. Which war? And then I lost my mind and went homestay in that great hills little belt mountains of Montana. I had to build my own schoolhouse and board the teacher. Had two kids of my own, my brother had two kids, the neighbor had two, didn't so much get there. So you worked in a mine, huh? And a sawmill too? Everything. Homesteaded on a farm, huh? I plowed on a bull tongue plow, ox teeth, mule teeth, horses. So would fit one dollar cord and now it's almost $40, not much difference. And when I left old Missouri at 17 years of age I'd never been shaved. My mother didn't cut in my hair. I went to a barbershop and paid five cents to get shaved. I didn't look any good ever since. I'd say so, Preston. You are looking good for 97. You look good. I was raised in a Methodist family. I'm named for Methodist ministry. The Mary Baptist girl lived there 46 years. My religion now, the way I'm living, watch me. And to give that good grace to the little cat person. Just keep living, that's religion enough. What kind of, what did you, what did you use to raise on your homestead there in Montana? I didn't raise anything on the homestead in Montana. I already had the four kids, that was enough. Over 5, 000 feet high there in the mountains. Snowed July 4th. The school teacher got $25 a month. We charged her $15 a month for boarding room. If it hadn't have been for groundhogs, we'd have starved to death. They killed a bear one year, killed a deer one, them none the neighbors would help. The neighbors would help you out, huh? I had such little schooling, like the nun, that I studied all my life. Educated myself. I have a wonderful memory. I could sit here and write a book not ask for question So he said he spent 50 years of your life in Idaho. What were you doing doing that? Works here for years with Mike Lewis. Yeah, I Work in Idaho. They need 15 Work three years and Mike Lewis is on the greenhouse And I really think that is 42 years, which I had three years ago. Been living in an apartment, doing my own cooking, 11 years. What are your sons and daughter doing now? I have five kids Two sons one lives In Clarkston Washington one lives in Los Angeles one daughter Anchorage, Alaska on the ramp in Washington, one at Seattle. I have been in every state of the nation, most of Canada, Mexico and Cuba. I've only rode on 32 airplanes. Callin killer I Wanted road on 32 airplanes Only had one lamb sheep in my life. About 20 hogs, six horses, six to five cattle. That was in Alberta, Canada. How long did you live in Alberta? I don't remember how many days. I had a very wonderful crop of oats. I didn't find it much of a breaker. Oats? A simple crop of oats, 120 bushels per acre. I had farmed in the big bend country of Washington. In Harvest Time we worked on Sunday. Up in Alberta, Canada, had a very wet season. Blew in lots of hay. It turned nice sunshine Fridays, Saturdays and Sundays. We worked Sundays. And they was going to have us arrested for working out beasts on Sundays against the law in Canada. I Was the only one that good hey to sell to that labor bond people that winter It was right Did you ever do any kind of mining No But Say you worked in the sawmill I Was working at home in 1902 in Puyallup, Washington, I Walked three miles for the job Got 15 cents an hour and dollar and a half for 10 hours. I was late four minutes one day and got fired at noon. I walked half a mile to another sawmill during the noon hour and got another job. Had to walk that extra mile every day. It was pretty heavy work, loading green railroad ties, living high in a boxcar. Yeah, that's work. Yeah, that works. Were they taking much timber out of Idaho back then in say 1902 around there. I know Idaho used to have quite a... You see, flat tire, one lawn. I don't know. You see a black car, one long. Sometimes two here and one there, three. That's a big. We lost the real Home In every mile he walks He cares a smile to talk Meeting on others on the way good morning here in say But I'll never get rid of it. You can take this one off. You have that, and it's just one of them. I've got hundreds of them. All right, I see you write poetry here. I write poetry all over the state of California. I write for eight publishers. Eight publishers? Would you like to read this poem? What? Would you like to read this poem? Read it how? Yeah, just read it. You read it? Oh, I'll get a news. I've got another. Well, I mean for recording. That's something I've long known as well. You write poetry all your life, have you? No, I started about 30 years ago. The first copyrighted poem that I ever written was a great poem on time. Invited to Montana. I had this type of copyrighted. Bruce copyrighted Good for his soul Yeah, it's good for his soul. I Didn't report that I'm gonna read a poem on that. Okay. I am Preston Phillips Johnson, a poet. And a poet. Here is one. For a million crow. Queen of the northwoods in Idaho. The white pine by the minion's grove. And these ferrutes are riches untold of nary soppers, copper and gold. Looking across a lovely lake, a boat, rowboat bobbin' the powerboat's way. It's easy to see run to pass, and pretty down your line, mine to last. Unto death and put it on your mind to last. And traveling by on your way, tap in, if you want to stay. Very good. And that's my story. And then they're good That's my story How's it sound? I came from Railroad, Goulda Springs, Missouri, the Ozark country. Springfield is a big city now that's 70 miles from where I live. I got there on the train 1897. Three days and three nights I landed at Spokane, Washington. I went to work then and We've been country near Wilbur wheat farm at $10 a month I Had 13 cows milk the whole day, light in the morning. Worked in the field all day. 13 cows of milk after night. But that was a fortune, $10 a month. You know where Mountain View, Missouri is not Mountain View, Missouri. Oh, yes That's still running. In the old Zarks we had 160 acres of land. They bought it in South Missouri land, come mid-war and down in the quarter.

lines.line: > Herb Folsom interviews Preston Phillips Johnson, who reflects on his arrival in Idaho 50 years ago during a bustling period for sawmills. Johnson narrates his experiences during the World War in Canada, losing a substantial amount of money, and his subsequent relocation to Montana, where he built a schoolhouse for local children.
** Herb Folsom, Preston Phillips Johnson, Idaho, sawmills, World War, Canada, Montana, schoolhouse **
keywords: ** Herb Folsom, Preston Phillips Johnson, Idaho, sawmills, World War, Canada, Montana, schoolhouse **

metadata.title: Introduction and Early Life
metadata.synopsis: Herb Folsom interviews Preston Phillips Johnson, who reflects on his arrival in Idaho 50 years ago during a bustling period for sawmills. Johnson narrates his experiences during the World War in Canada, losing a substantial amount of money, and his subsequent relocation to Montana, where he built a schoolhouse for local children.
metadata.keywords: Herb Folsom, Preston Phillips Johnson, Idaho, sawmills, World War, Canada, Montana, schoolhouse

lines.line: # Life in Missouri and Personal Anecdotes

> Preston shares vivid stories about his youth in Missouri, working in sawmills and farming with different animals. He humorously recounts his first shave and mentions his family's religious background. He also touches on family life and his experience with the July snowfall in Montana.
** Missouri, sawmills, farm, religious background, family life, Montana, snowfall **
keywords: ** Missouri, sawmills, farm, religious background, family life, Montana, snowfall **

lines.line: > Preston speaks about his diverse careers, including his work in sawmills and farming in Alberta, with a notable oat crop yield. He shares how he adapted to challenges, like being fired and finding another job quickly. His self-education and poetry writing are explored, highlighting his publishing success.
** careers, sawmills, Alberta, oat crop, job, self-education, poetry, publishing **

lines.line: > Preston concludes with his origins from Missouri and his early travels to Washington. He revisits his early work on wheat farms, experiencing a challenging but rewarding era, and reflects on the land his family owned in Missouri.
